title: Farmhouse Bed for 18" Dolls
summary: A miniature farmhouse-style bed sized for an 18" doll, built the same way as its full-size counterparts — slatted panel headboard and footboard, siderails, and a plywood platform.
description:
This is the full-size farmhouse bed's construction shrunk down rather than simplified: a slatted panel gets its bottom board centered and screwed on first, then the legs, then a top board — the same sequence, just at doll scale, right down to countersinking every screw before driving it. Resting the panel boards on a scrap of thin plywood while lining up the bottom board is what keeps that centering step accurate at a size where a 1/16" error is actually visible.

A third siderail support down the center is worth adding for any child who plays rough with dolls, and more supports still if the platform is cut from something flimsier than plywood — advice worth taking literally, since a doll bed sees more enthusiastic handling per square inch than most furniture in the house.

tools.note: Countersinks every screw before driving it.

materials.name: Plywood scrap, 1/4"
materials.note: Sturdy cardboard also works for the platform.

materials.name: Wood screws, 1-1/4", self-tapping or brad nails
Wood screws, 2", self-tapping or brad nails

steps.title: Cut and label the parts
steps.body: Cut everything first and label it, because at this scale the pieces look alike and a 6" board mixed in with a 3" one is easy to do. From the 1x3 (2 1/2" wide), cut five headboard panel boards 6" long, five footboard panel boards 3" long, two caps 16 1/2" long, and two siderails 19" long. From the 2x2, cut two headboard legs 11" and two footboard legs 4 1/4". From the 1x2 (1 1/2" wide), cut two bottom-of-panel boards 12 1/2", two top-of-panel boards 15 1/2", and two bed frame pieces 14". The numbers check each other: five 2 1/2" panel boards side by side make a 12 1/2" panel, which is why the bottom boards are 12 1/2"; add a 1 1/2" leg at each side and you get the 15 1/2" top boards, and the 16 1/2" caps then overhang 1/2" at each end.

steps.title: Line out the panel boards
steps.body: Lay the five 6" headboard panel boards face down with their long edges tight together on a scrap of thin plywood; the plywood holds their faces in one plane while you work, which is what makes the centring accurate at a size where a 1/16" of drift is visible. Set the 12 1/2" bottom-of-panel board across them so it covers all five evenly with no overhang at either end, since the 12 1/2" board matches the panel's 12 1/2" width exactly. Mark and predrill one hole into each panel board through the bottom board, using the countersink bit so every head sits below the surface and fills clean.

steps.title: Attach the bottom panel board
steps.body: Run glue along the bottom board where it crosses the five panel boards and drive a 1-1/4" screw into each one through the predrilled countersunk holes. Work from the middle board outward so the panel cannot creep sideways as you go, and keep the five boards pressed tight together - any gap you leave here stays in the finished headboard.
materials: Wood screws, 1-1/4", self-tapping or brad nails

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Stand an 11" 2x2 headboard leg against each end of the panel, flush with the panel's outer edge, and predrill and countersink through the leg into both the panel boards and the end of the 12 1/2" bottom board. Drive 2" screws with glue; the longer screw is needed because it has to cross the 1 1/2" leg before it reaches anything. Check the legs are square to the panel and parallel to each other - the panel plus two legs should measure 15 1/2" across, which is exactly the length of the top-of-panel board that goes on next.
materials: Wood screws, 2", self-tapping or brad nails

steps.title: Attach the top panel board
steps.body: Set the 15 1/2" top-of-panel board across the front so it spans the panel and both legs with its ends flush to the legs' outer faces. Predrill, countersink, and fasten with 1-1/4" screws or brad nails and glue, one into each panel board and one into each leg. Keep it parallel to the bottom board: those two boards together are what read as the farmhouse rail-and-stile look at this scale.
materials: Wood screws, 1-1/4", self-tapping or brad nails

steps.title: Cap the headboard
steps.body: Lay the 16 1/2" 1x3 cap across the top of the headboard, on the end grain of the two legs and the panel boards, centred so it overhangs 1/2" at each end of the 15 1/2" assembly. Predrill and countersink two screws down into the legs, add glue, and drive them home. Check both overhangs with the tape before the glue grabs, because an off-centre cap is the first thing anyone notices on a piece this small.
materials: Wood screws, 1-1/4", self-tapping or brad nails

steps.title: Build the footboard
steps.body: Build the footboard exactly the same way with the shorter parts: five 3" panel boards screwed to the second 12 1/2" bottom board, then the two 4 1/4" 2x2 legs, then the second 15 1/2" top board, then the second 16 1/2" cap. It ends up the same 15 1/2" wide as the headboard and much shorter, which is what makes the bed read correctly from the side. Countersink every screw here too; filled and sanded flush, they disappear under paint.
materials: Wood screws, 1-1/4", self-tapping or brad nails

steps.title: Attach the siderails
steps.body: Screw the two 19" siderails to the ends of the two 14" bed frame pieces to make a rectangle. With the 14" pieces between the rails and the 3/4" rails outside them, the frame comes out 15 1/2" across, which is the same width as the headboard and footboard it has to meet. Predrill and countersink every hole, because 3/4" pine splits at the ends at this scale. Check the frame's diagonals match before going on, and if the bed is heading for a child who plays hard, or the platform will be cardboard rather than plywood, add a third support across the middle now while the frame is still open.
materials: Wood screws, 1-1/4", self-tapping or brad nails

steps.title: Assemble and finish
steps.body: Stand the headboard and footboard up and predrill and screw the frame's siderail ends into the inside faces of the legs with 2" screws, setting the frame at whatever height you want the mattress to sit. Cut the 1/4" plywood platform to drop inside the frame and lay it in; measure the frame you actually built rather than working from a number, since this is the last part and it only has to fit what is in front of you. Fill the countersunk holes, sand everything smooth and round the corners, then paint or finish and let it cure fully before the doll moves in.
materials: Wood screws, 2", self-tapping or brad nails

images.alt: Miniature farmhouse-style bed for an 18-inch doll
